title: Momentum and Impulse
learningObjectives: Apply conservation of momentum in collisions
Distinguish between elastic and inelastic collisions
introText: Momentum is a measure of the 'quantity of motion' of an object. The principle of conservation of momentum is one of the most powerful tools in mechanics.

steps.heading: Linear Momentum
steps.body:
Momentum (p) is defined as:
p = mv

where m is mass (kg) and v is velocity (m/s). Momentum is a vector quantity with units of kg·m/s.

The total momentum of a system is conserved when no external net force acts on it.
steps.deepDive: Newton's second law can be written as F = dp/dt. If no external force acts (F = 0), then dp/dt = 0, meaning momentum is constant (conserved).

steps.heading: Impulse
steps.body:
Impulse (J) is the change in momentum caused by a force over time:

J = FΔt = Δp = m(v − u)

Impulse is measured in N·s (= kg·m/s). A large force over a short time can produce the same impulse as a small force over a long time.

This explains why safety features (airbags, crumple zones) extend collision time to reduce the peak force.
steps.deepDive: The area under a force-time graph equals the impulse. This is useful when force varies with time.

steps.heading: Conservation of Momentum and Collisions
steps.body:
For two objects colliding:
m₁u₁ + m₂u₂ = m₁v₁ + m₂v₂

Elastic collision: both momentum AND kinetic energy are conserved.
Inelastic collision: only momentum is conserved (KE is lost to heat/sound/deformation).
Perfectly inelastic: objects stick together (maximum KE loss).

Explosions: objects start together and separate (momentum still conserved).
steps.deepDive: Elastic collisions between equal masses result in an exchange of velocities. This is seen in Newton's cradle.

steps.questionText: A 3 kg object moving at 4 m/s collides with a stationary 1 kg object. They stick together. What is their combined velocity after the collision?
steps.explanation: Conservation of momentum: 3 × 4 + 1 × 0 = (3 + 1) × v. 12 = 4v, v = 3 m/s.

questions.questionText: A 0.1 kg ball hits a wall at 10 m/s and bounces back at 8 m/s. What is the magnitude of the impulse on the ball?
